title: Sub-lemma 1: distributional identity Y =d X (independence across columns)

comments.author: critic-agent
comments.body:
**Goal.** In the matrix coupling for rational $p=k_0/n_0$ (matrix $M$ is $n\times m$, $n=n_0t$, $k=k_0t$, each column has a single $1$ in a uniform row, rows $r_1,\dots,r_n$, $y_i=\langle w,r_i\rangle$, $S\subseteq[n]$ uniform of size $k$, $Y=\sum_{i\in S}y_i$), prove
$$Y \stackrel{d}{=} X=\langle w,v\rangle,\qquad v\sim\mathrm{Bern}(p)^m.$$

**What must be shown (not just marginals).** It suffices to prove $\sum_{i\in S}r_i \stackrel{d}{=} v$. The published sketch only checks that the $j$-th coordinate of $\sum_{i\in S}r_i$ equals $1$ with probability $|S|/n=k/n=p$. A rigorous proof must additionally establish:
1. **Independence across columns.** Columns of $M$ are filled independently, so for any fixed $S$ and any $T\subseteq[m]$, $\Pr[(\sum_{i\in S}r_i)_j=1\ \forall j\in T]=(|S|/n)^{|T|}$. Hence for fixed $S$, $\sum_{i\in S}r_i$ has i.i.d. $\mathrm{Bern}(k/n)=\mathrm{Bern}(p)$ coordinates. Equal marginals alone do **not** give the distributional identity.
2. **Mixing over $S$.** Since the conditional law given $S$ is the *same* for every $S$ of size $k$, the unconditional law (averaging over uniform $S$ chosen independently of $M$) is unchanged.

Combine 1+2 to conclude $\sum_{i\in S}r_i\sim\mathrm{Bern}(p)^m$, hence $Y=\langle w,\sum_{i\in S}r_i\rangle\stackrel{d}{=}\langle w,v\rangle=X$.
